# Transcrição: 04-Employee Buy-In- Why People Love Storytelling

- **Fonte:** 04-Employee Buy-In- Why People Love Storytelling
- **Duração:** 04:27
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

All right, here's some good news. Employee buy-in, don't even worry about it. It's going to be a piece of cake. I have been through thousands of hours of training and coaching and workshops and professional development, so I know how awful most of it is. When it comes to storytelling, however, your employees are going to love it. The reason is going to be simple. Very quickly, your employees are going to begin to understand that what we're teaching them not only applies to their business life, but it applies to their personal life as well. In fact, the biggest struggle I have often when working with a company is keeping the employees actually focused on storytelling for business. During every break we have, they'll often come up to me and ask me about questions about storytelling and relationship to dating in terms of interviewing, getting their kids to listen to them. They become deeply invested in storytelling because they quickly see that this is not a business skill, that this is a life skill, that if they become better storytellers, they could actually get their brother-in-law to finally shut up at the Thanksgiving Day table, or they might be able to use storytelling to make a new friend. They might use storytelling to get their grandchildren to finally listen to them for the first time in their lives. They will quickly understand that the training you're offering them, this personal development in storytelling is going to change so many aspects of their lives. And it's absolutely true. I am the worst golfer I have ever met. I have never actually played golf with a person who was worse than me, other than my nine-year-old son, and he's catching up quickly. I am a horrendous golfer and yet every weekend, without fail, I have a multitude of people to play golf with. Why? It's definitely not because of the way I hit the ball. What my friends know is that after I hit my ball and it shanks into the woods, and we all have to head off into the woods to look for Matt's ball, on the way to the woods, I'm going to tell them a good story. It might be something that just happened five minutes ago before I arrived at the golf course or last week, or I might tell them a story about when I was six years old. Whatever it is, they know they're going to be entertained with a story. Your employees are going to figure that out really quickly as well. And they're going to be excited about this training that they're receiving, not only because it's going to help them in all the things you want them to do for business, but it's going to change the other side of their lives as well. They're going to understand that they've been telling stories all their lives, but they haven't been telling them well. They'll also quickly understand that it doesn't take much to begin telling great stories, and then they will quickly make the connection how storytelling can change their life. I often find myself coming into companies and sitting in front of a bunch of people, and invariably there's people in the room with their arms folded. With that look, that look of distrust and disdain, why am I here?

## [03:02 - 04:27]

I have a million things to do. Who is this person and why am I learning this? But I'll tell you this, within 10 minutes, without question, those arms aren't crossed anymore. People are leaning forward and they're ready to learn. That is the beauty of storytelling. A few years ago, I was giving a talk to a large medical organization. It was sort of like a two hour workshop. At the end of the workshop, I went and I sat down and the CEO of the company sat down right beside me and she said, when my people told me that I was going to sit through a two hour lecture on storytelling, I wanted to kill them. I wanted to fire them on the spot. And she said, and when you began talking, I thought, what is he doing and why am I here listening to it? But then she told me, within 10 minutes, you had me. She said, I want to be able to do the same thing that you just did. That is a CEO buying into professional development that her employees understood before she did. But by the end of that workshop, everyone was on board and we were moving forward with storytelling in that organization. So if you're sitting there worried about how you're going to get your employees to engage in this training, if you're worried about how they're going to be as excited about it as you might be right now, don't worry. Employee buy-in when it comes to storytelling is very simple because it's storytelling and it's useful for everyone.
